name: First-Degree Heart Block
identifying_features: Each P wave followed by QRS
treatment_notes: Usually benign and requires no treatment. Monitor for progression to higher-degree blocks. Review medications that slow AV conduction.
clinical_significance: Generally benign. The AV node conducts every impulse but with a delay. Can be a normal finding in athletes.

name: ROSC Detection
identifying_features: ETCO2 rises from arrest levels (<10-20) to >40 mmHg
Most reliable early indicator of ROSC
associated_conditions: Return of spontaneous circulation during CPR
treatment_notes: Check pulse to confirm ROSC. Initiate post-cardiac arrest care. Targeted temperature management. Hemodynamic optimization. 12-lead ECG. Consider cath lab.
clinical_significance: A sudden sustained rise in ETCO2 (typically >40 mmHg) during CPR is the earliest and most reliable indicator of ROSC — often detected before a pulse is palpable.

name: Hyperventilation Pattern
identifying_features: Normal waveform shape but short and shallow
treatment_notes: Reduce ventilation rate to 10-12/min. Coach breathing if anxiety-related. Investigate underlying cause. Avoid over-ventilation in traumatic brain injury (target ETCO2 35-40).
clinical_significance: Low ETCO2 from hyperventilation causes cerebral vasoconstriction and decreased cerebral blood flow. Critical to avoid in TBI patients where ETCO2 targets are 35-40 mmHg.

name: Third-Degree (Complete) Heart Block

clinical_annotations.qrsDuration: Wide if ventricular escape, narrow if junctional escape

identifying_features: AV dissociation — P waves march through independently
Regular atrial rate, regular ventricular rate
treatment_notes: Transcutaneous pacing immediately if symptomatic. Atropine may help if junctional escape. Dopamine/epinephrine drip. Transvenous pacing. Permanent pacemaker.
regularity: Regular P-P intervals, regular R-R intervals, but independent of each other
clinical_significance: Complete failure of AV conduction. Potentially lethal — patient depends entirely on escape rhythm for cardiac output.

name: Second-Degree Heart Block Type I (Wenckebach)
treatment_notes: Usually benign; often transient. Monitor closely. Atropine if symptomatic. Rarely requires pacing. Observe for progression to higher-degree block.
clinical_significance: Usually occurs at AV node level (supra-Hisian). Generally has a benign course. 'Longer, longer, longer, drop — that's a Wenckebach!'

name: Premature Ventricular Complexes (PVCs)
identifying_features: No preceding P wave for PVC
T wave deflection opposite to QRS
treatment_notes: Isolated PVCs usually benign — treat underlying cause. Frequent PVCs (>6/min, couplets, R-on-T) may need amiodarone or lidocaine. If hemodynamically significant, treat as VT.
clinical_significance: Common and often benign. Concerning when frequent, multifocal, in couplets, or R-on-T phenomenon. Can trigger VT/VFib.

name: Ventricular Tachycardia (Polymorphic / Torsades de Pointes)
identifying_features: Twisting of QRS axis around baseline
treatment_notes: Pulseless: defibrillation. Magnesium sulfate 2g IV over 5-20 min. Correct electrolyte abnormalities. Overdrive pacing to increase rate and shorten QT. Stop offending medications.
clinical_significance: A specific form of polymorphic VT associated with prolonged QT interval. Different treatment than standard VT — magnesium is first-line.

name: Normal Sinus Rhythm
identifying_features: Each P wave followed by QRS
Upright P waves in Lead II
treatment_notes: No treatment required — this is the normal cardiac rhythm.
clinical_significance: Normal baseline rhythm. All intervals within normal limits. This is the standard against which all other rhythms are compared.

name: STEMI — Anterior Wall

clinical_annotations.reciprocal: ST depression in II, III, aVF

identifying_features: Reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads
May have pathological Q waves in V1-V4
treatment_notes: Activate cath lab — PCI within 90 minutes. Aspirin 324mg chewed. Heparin. Nitroglycerin (avoid if RV involvement). Morphine for pain. 12-lead ECG serial monitoring.
clinical_significance: Large territory of myocardium at risk. Anterior STEMI has highest mortality. Can develop cardiogenic shock, CHF, VT/VFib.

name: Esophageal Intubation

clinical_annotations.interpretation: No CO2 detected = not in trachea

identifying_features: May see brief CO2 from gastric air (diminishes rapidly)
treatment_notes: Immediately remove tube and re-intubate. Ventilate with BVM while preparing. Confirm tracheal placement with bilateral breath sounds, chest rise, AND sustained ETCO2.
clinical_significance: Capnography is the gold standard for confirming ET tube placement. Absence of CO2 waveform after 6 breaths confirms esophageal placement. A brief CO2 reading from gastric air will rapidly diminish.

name: STEMI — Inferior Wall
identifying_features: ST elevation in II, III, aVF
Reciprocal ST depression in I, aVL
Check right-sided leads (V4R) for RV involvement
treatment_notes: Same as anterior STEMI protocol. If RV involved: avoid nitrates, give IV fluids for preload. Watch for bradycardia (RCA supplies SA/AV nodes).
clinical_significance: RCA supplies the inferior wall and often the SA/AV nodes. Inferior STEMI may present with bradycardia, AV blocks, and RV infarction.

name: PEA (Pulseless Electrical Activity)
identifying_features: Can look like any organized rhythm
Diagnosis requires pulse check — ECG alone cannot distinguish
treatment_notes: CPR. Epinephrine 1mg IV q3-5min. NOT a shockable rhythm. Aggressively search for and treat reversible causes (H's and T's). Consider point-of-care ultrasound.
clinical_significance: Electrical activity without mechanical function. Treatment success depends on identifying and reversing the underlying cause. PEA is a clinical diagnosis, not purely an ECG finding.

name: Ventricular Fibrillation
identifying_features: No identifiable P waves, QRS, or T waves
Coarse VFib has larger amplitude than fine VFib
treatment_notes: Immediate defibrillation. CPR. Epinephrine 1mg IV q3-5min. Amiodarone 300mg IV (first dose), then 150mg IV. Treat reversible causes (H's and T's).
clinical_significance: Cardiac arrest rhythm — no effective cardiac output. Most common initial rhythm in sudden cardiac arrest. Survival depends on early defibrillation.

name: Normal Capnography Waveform
identifying_features: Alveolar plateau with slight upward slope
treatment_notes: Normal waveform — no intervention needed. Use as baseline for comparison.
clinical_significance: Confirms proper ET tube placement and adequate ventilation. ETCO2 correlates with PaCO2 in patients with normal V/Q matching.

name: Bronchospasm (Shark Fin)
treatment_notes: Administer bronchodilators (albuterol, ipratropium). Monitor response — waveform should become more rectangular with effective treatment. Consider ET tube repositioning if mechanical obstruction.
clinical_significance: The 'shark fin' waveform is pathognomonic for obstructive airway disease. Serial waveform monitoring shows treatment response — improving waveform shape indicates bronchodilator effectiveness.

name: Asystole
identifying_features: No P waves, QRS complexes, or T waves
treatment_notes: CPR. Epinephrine 1mg IV q3-5min. NOT a shockable rhythm — do NOT defibrillate. Search for and treat reversible causes (H's and T's). Consider termination of resuscitation.
clinical_significance: Non-shockable cardiac arrest rhythm. Carries the worst prognosis of all arrest rhythms. Always confirm in at least 2 leads to rule out fine VFib.

name: Sinus Tachycardia
identifying_features: P waves may merge with preceding T waves at high rates
treatment_notes: Treat the underlying cause. Do NOT cardiovert sinus tachycardia. Volume resuscitation if hypovolemic. Antipyretics for fever. Analgesia for pain.
clinical_significance: A compensatory response. Always search for and treat the underlying cause rather than the tachycardia itself.

name: Junctional Rhythm

clinical_annotations.prInterval: < 0.12 sec (if P wave visible) or absent

identifying_features: Inverted P waves in II, III, aVF (if visible)
P wave may be before, during, or after QRS
treatment_notes: If asymptomatic, observe. If symptomatic: Atropine 0.5mg IV. Consider pacing if persistent bradycardia. Treat underlying cause.
clinical_significance: AV junction assumes pacemaker role when SA node fails or is too slow. A junctional escape rhythm is a safety mechanism.

name: STEMI — Lateral Wall

clinical_annotations.culpritArtery: LCx (Left Circumflex) or diagonal branch of LAD

identifying_features: ST elevation in I, aVL, V5, V6
Reciprocal ST depression in III, aVF
treatment_notes: Standard STEMI protocol with PCI. Lateral STEMI may be subtle — high lateral (I, aVL only) can be missed.
clinical_significance: Lateral wall involvement. High lateral STEMI (I, aVL only) is easily overlooked. Always check all 12 leads systematically.

name: Atrial Flutter
identifying_features: Sawtooth flutter waves (best in II, III, aVF, V1)
Ventricular rate depends on conduction ratio (2:1, 3:1, 4:1)
Regular ventricular rhythm if fixed block
treatment_notes: Rate control with calcium channel blockers or beta-blockers. Synchronized cardioversion (50-100J) if unstable. Ablation is definitive treatment.
rate: Atrial ~300, Ventricular depends on block ratio
clinical_significance: Organized macro-reentrant atrial circuit. A ventricular rate of ~150 bpm should always raise suspicion for atrial flutter with 2:1 block.

name: Ventricular Tachycardia (Monomorphic)
treatment_notes: Pulseless VT: defibrillation, CPR, epinephrine, amiodarone per ACLS. Stable VT with pulse: amiodarone 150mg IV over 10 min. Unstable with pulse: synchronized cardioversion (100J).
clinical_significance: Life-threatening arrhythmia. Can rapidly deteriorate into VFib. Pulseless VT treated as cardiac arrest.

name: Second-Degree Heart Block Type II (Mobitz II)
identifying_features: Constant PR interval for conducted beats
Sudden dropped QRS without PR prolongation
treatment_notes: More dangerous than Type I. Transcutaneous pacing standby. Atropine may be ineffective (infra-nodal block). Transvenous pacing often required. Permanent pacemaker indicated.
rate: Atrial regular, ventricular regular or irregular
clinical_significance: Occurs below the AV node (infra-Hisian). High risk of progressing to complete heart block. Requires pacemaker placement.

name: Sinus Bradycardia
treatment_notes: If symptomatic (hypotension, altered mental status): Atropine 0.5mg IV q3-5min (max 3mg). Consider transcutaneous pacing. Dopamine or epinephrine infusion if atropine ineffective.
clinical_significance: May be normal in athletes or during sleep. Concerning when causing symptoms of poor perfusion.

name: SVT (Supraventricular Tachycardia)
identifying_features: P waves often buried in QRS or T wave
associated_conditions: Often in otherwise healthy young adults
treatment_notes: Vagal maneuvers first (bearing down, carotid massage). Adenosine 6mg rapid IV push, repeat 12mg if needed. If unstable: synchronized cardioversion (50-100J).
clinical_significance: Paroxysmal: sudden onset and offset. Usually not life-threatening but can cause hemodynamic instability if sustained.

name: Atrial Fibrillation
treatment_notes: Rate control: Diltiazem, beta-blockers, or digoxin. If unstable with rapid ventricular response: synchronized cardioversion. Anticoagulation for stroke prevention (CHA2DS2-VASc score).
clinical_significance: Most common sustained arrhythmia. Loss of atrial kick reduces cardiac output ~25%. Major risk factor for stroke.
